title: What Is a Scientific Hypothesis? (Hint: It's Not Just an Educated Guess)
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You've probably heard that a hypothesis is an 'educated guess.' That's not wrong, but it's incomplete. A real scientific hypothesis is specific, testable, and falsifiable. Here's what that actually means and why it matters for your science grade.

The 'Educated Guess' Misconception

A guess is 'I think the plant will grow.' An educated guess is 'I think the plant will grow because plants need sunlight.' A hypothesis is 'Bean plants grown under red light will grow at least 20% taller than bean plants grown under green light, because chlorophyll absorbs red light more efficiently.'

See the difference? The hypothesis has a specific prediction (20% taller), a comparison (red vs. green), a measurable outcome (height), and a scientific reason (chlorophyll absorption). That's what makes it scientific.

The Three Requirements of a Good Hypothesis

1. It must be specific. 'The plant will grow better' is vague. 'The plant will grow 15-20% taller' is specific. A specific hypothesis tells you what success looks like.

2. It must be testable. 'Plants grow better with love' is not testable. How do you measure love? 'Plants grow better with classical music' is testable. You can play music to some plants and not others, then measure growth.

3. It must be falsifiable. This is the one most students miss. A hypothesis must be able to be proven wrong. If there's no possible result that would disprove your hypothesis, it's not scientific. 'All swans are white' is falsifiable (one black swan disproves it). 'All swans are beautiful' is not falsifiable (beauty is subjective).

The If... Then... Because... Format

Use this template for every hypothesis you write in science class:

'If [I change this variable], then [this outcome will happen], because [this scientific reason].'

Example: 'If bean plants are grown under green light, then they will grow slower than plants under white light, because chlorophyll does not absorb green light efficiently.'

This format forces you to be specific, testable, and falsifiable. It also shows your teacher that you understand the science behind your prediction.

Hypothesis vs. Prediction vs. Theory

Students often confuse these terms. Here's the distinction:

Hypothesis: A specific, testable prediction about the outcome of an experiment. 'Drinking coffee before a test will improve scores.'

Prediction: A statement about what will happen in a specific situation. 'If I drink coffee, I will get a higher score.' This is part of a hypothesis but not the whole thing.

Theory: A broad, well-supported explanation for a wide range of phenomena. Germ theory, evolution, and plate tectonics are theories. They're supported by mountains of evidence. A hypothesis can become a theory after extensive testing, but a theory is not 'just a guess.'

Common Hypothesis Mistakes

Mistake 1: The circular hypothesis. 'Plants will grow better with fertilizer because fertilizer helps plants grow.' That's just restating the same idea. The 'because' needs a mechanism. 'Because fertilizer adds nitrogen, which plants need to produce chlorophyll.'

Mistake 2: The unmeasurable hypothesis. 'Students will learn better with the new method.' Better how? Test scores? Retention after a week? Speed of completion? Specify your measurement.

Mistake 3: The directionless hypothesis. 'The temperature will affect bacterial growth.' Will it increase or decrease growth? Predict a direction. 'Higher temperatures will increase bacterial growth up to a point.'

Why Your Hypothesis Matters for Your Grade

Teachers look for three things in a hypothesis: specificity, testability, and falsifiability. If you nail these, the rest of your lab report is easier. Your hypothesis guides your procedure (what you measure), your results (what data you collect), and your conclusion (whether your hypothesis was supported).

A good hypothesis is the backbone of a good experiment. Start there, and everything else falls into place.
